Alright, we're up to step 16 now. Knock them off the fence. This is a pretty important step and we're getting really close to the final part of the program. Then knock them off the fence. You want to reinforce the value. You want to jack up the must have emotional need of your sales message. Now you do this by having stimulating surprises that change the entire conversation. This is the time you would introduce a bonus or something special, something you'll do, maybe a group phone call, maybe you'll do a consultation, maybe you'll give them a free book, maybe you'll show up and mow their lawn for a year. I don't know, but something that just suddenly changes everything from focusing on where you have been going to suddenly, wow, this becomes a very, very, very good deal. One of the ways to do that is to just start piling on stuff. Unexpected, it's your birthday, rare treat dumps. In other words, a lot of stuff. Say your back catalog. We'll give you everything in our archives. We will give you the complete series of books that I've used originally to get where I am today. Whatever it is that just seems like such an overwhelming value. Free stuff up the yin and yang, piling higher and higher. One of the classic ways that old school direct response marketers used to do this step would be to just make a lot of different free reports. Free reports are kind of an easy thing for you to do. If you are an expert in what you're doing and your prospect is not an expert, and one of the reasons he's buying from you is that he wants to know what you know, there's a lot of things that you may be taking for granted that you can do a special report on. You can do, special reports can be five pages long or they can be 500 pages long or you can break them up. But basically, the free report, it's not the fact that you're getting a free report. It's what the free report imparts. The title of the thing makes all the difference in the world. It's the idea. It's the sharing of information that you have that your prospect doesn't have. You can have 15 free reports. I've seen old school marketers do this a lot. Each one is very, very valuable. They may have been together in a book and it would be one book, but as 15 different reports, they carry a different kind of value. It's like literally a stack of free reports. Even if you're doing it virtually, you can still talk about them as free reports. If they were printed, it would be a certain amount of pages, things like that. Basically you want to make the case that you have better, more, stronger, longer, everything that you can say more about. So whatever the offer is up to this point, you want to start just jacking it up. The whole idea is an overwhelming offer made even more overwhelming. So the question about the value that I get if I buy what you have, the service or product that you have, is no longer a question because I'm getting so much stuff. The purpose of this is to obliterate the last lingering objections. Salesmen know that it's really easy to get someone on the fence. By that I mean they'll say, you know, I agree with you. That's a pretty good product or service. And I think someday down the line, I'm actually going to take you up on that and I'm going to buy it because I think you've done a good job and I trust you and blah, blah, blah. But they're not buying it. That's on the fence. What the really true good salesman knows how to do is finish the job and knock him off the fence. That's the metaphor. So that the person doesn't say, maybe sometime down the road, I'll get this. He says, how do I get this now? I need it right now. And you do that by understanding what needs to be said, what needs to be given to him, or what needs to be taken away from him to finalize the deal. The take away involves limitations. It's like you only have a certain amount of time. There's only a certain number of reports. There's only a certain number of opportunities here. So this is what we're talking about. You want to pile on, bring out the heavy artillery and get serious about limits about how short a time period there is. And so when the last objections are about, I'm almost there and I'm not quite ready, then you want to say, well, you're going to get this, this, this, and this, and you only got a certain amount of time and all of this. So some examples would be like the first 10 buyers get, or the first 100, or the first 1,000 buyers get something of enormous value. There's a limited number of these bonuses that you have. Or the bonuses could be reports, free phone consultations, maybe a ticket to an event. A lot of people put on events, especially during launches, except they limit, you know, only the first number of people or the first people to establish some level of participation will get to come to this event. And the event is then touted and brought up and explained, and that becomes something you must, you know, must be at event. Early bird pricing going up at a specific time. The price will be X until 10 days from now, then it goes up to X plus Y. And the clock ticking down, you know, this is only available for the next 24 hours. Nope, the next 23 hours and 50 seconds. Nope, the next 23 hours and 45 seconds. That's, a lot of this stuff works. Now there's two kinds of bonuses that you want to put out. One is more of the same. And that would be adding to the benefits of the original deal. For a copywriting offer, say, for copyright and coaching, I would add interviews with the top players in the niche, like other copiers. I would interview other copywriters or other marketers so that would be more of the same, more of what I'm doing at another level up. Or I might offer a critique of your ad, staying within that. The other kind of bonus is tangential, which means it's close to or supporting of the original deal, but not part of it. So you add something deep on lead generation, for example, or on productivity, or on deal making, or on lifestyle. You know, you could have a free report on what to do with the new money and the new extra amount of time you're going to have when you're rich and not working very much. You know, how are you going to handle that? How are you going to invest your new money that you're going to have if you're selling, say, an information product and how to do well in the stock market? So those are the two kinds of bonuses. Definitely pay attention to this part of the program. We're getting close to the actual close. The whole idea is to finalize the deal here. It's really easy to get people really close to buying, but then they won't buy if they aren't absolutely convinced that they need to do it now, and this is the time to pull the trigger. All right. Knock them off the fence, Q&A. More examples and questions this time. Things that people have put up, and there's been, say, back and forth with their coach. So we've culled through a lot of those, and I'm just going to throw some common themes, questions, things that people have gotten stuck on for a little bit. The big question is, how important is this? A lot of people, a lot of effort went into creating the product, and now they're excited with the product, and now you throw up, oh, wait, you have a bonus too. So as far as taking something to market, talk about the, when it comes to knock them off the fence, how critical is this stacking the offer or adding bonus? It's actually very, very critical, and a lot of marketers, you can't wait till the last minute to do this stuff. However, it needs to be super valuable. Now, one of the ways you can do this is mine the resources that you have. If you really are an expert or you really are a go-to person in your particular niche, then you've got a network of people you could interview, and it doesn't have to be fancy. A lot of these free reports, when they were written, they would literally be, John, you know, blah, blah, blah, Stan, well, there's this question, John, and you can just transcribe them in five to 50 pages of transcription. The early NLP books were just transcriptions of conversations between the principles. And it's like, it may not seem valuable to you, but it's extremely valuable to the people that aren't on the inside of your world. You can go through your phone book and look at people who, anybody you've dealt with, your mentors, other people who do what you do, your competitors. I've even interviewed competitors, and they know that the more that you make the market bigger, they're going to benefit also. I've done interview series with people, just pick up the phone and have the same kind of conversation I have with them anyway, except I recorded it, got their permission and used that. That was the Scuttlebud series. The job of the bonuses, again, is to knock them off the fence. Your product may be spectacular, may be great, but after a certain point, it is still just the product. Now you're saying, oh my God, it's the product and I get all of this. And again, I think I mentioned this in a previous Q&A. I worked for a number of clients whose main product was kind of, was good, but there was nothing sexy about it. So we sold the bonuses. We sold the interviews. We sold the special reports. And then they were so valuable that at the end, all we had to say was easy to get these things. They're all free. Just subscribe for one year to this boring newsletter, this guy has, and you'll get all this free stuff. And that's a very standard tactic. Sometimes the bonuses become the thing that people talk about. Two of the bonuses I've had are, what was it, the quick marketing fixes, which was a 10-page report I think I did several years ago, which was basically breaking down what I did in my hour-long consultations on the phone with people. And I just broke it down into 10 sections about the questions that I asked people to try to get them centered and try to get their business moving and get unstuck. And I called it 10 quick marketing fixes, and that's been a standard bonus for a long time. And people have, it's jump-started some people's careers. It's very short. It was just something I tossed off, but it was very, very valuable to a number of people. The Power Words report, I just went through a lot of my pieces and pulled out the action verbs and a lot of the power phrases and stuff and came up with a very, very long report. I think it's 40 pages, somewhere between 20 and 40 pages. But it didn't take me a long time, but it was extremely valuable. And it's one of the most doggier reports that people have on their desk. A lot of, almost every writer I've talked to has a copy of that. Yeah, I would say that's a good example of something like the genius, simple and genius both. Because you said you had it there, and that, if you were to search online for the most commented on people years later, I still use this, that comes up probably more than the main products. Because it was handy, it quick reference, and it has long-term perceived value. Yes. And for someone slightly on the outside, no one's a writer who wanted to be able to write like me, this was an easy way to access John's brain in a way. So you could have special reports. How to get on the inside of this business, how to understand what me and the other guys like me are thinking and doing. Because again, if you're the go-to guy, if you're the person who has the information that your prospect doesn't have, then any stuff you provide is going to be very valuable. Now some of the stuff you may want to sell, especially in information marketing, people have done books and things that were courses that were worth money at one time. Later, as the competition got stiffer and as what you used to sell now became offered for free, they became special reports. So sometimes your old stuff goes up on the shelf and you never access it again. This is where you can pull that off the shelf and you can say, hey, we sold a ton of this years ago. I'm going to give it to you for free now. There's a number of the marketers we know that have been in the business for a long time who are just essentially making a product dump of their old catalog. And it's all great stuff. It's just a little bit dated and it used to go for a lot of money and now it's because so many people have knocked it off and stuff, it's less valuable. But it's still super valuable stuff. So there's almost, if you know anything about your market that other people don't know, you are just a walking font of resources that all you have to do is sit down and think, what if I shared my Rolodex with somebody? How valuable would that be? Now you don't want to do that, but how about interviewing the people in your Rolodex? People know what a Rolodex is now. If you're under 40, they're called contact lists now. So go through your contact list and say, you know what? I talked to Bob, the engineer almost every week and he gives me great tips on how to do X, Y, and Z. How about if I record Bob and do a special thing on Bob? How valuable would that be to someone who doesn't know who Bob is and doesn't have access to him? It'd be worth a lot. Yeah, another way we've helped people do this in the past is unbundled the product. Especially if it's a book or an information product or if it comes with a physical thing that you perceive the attachments or the other things you clip on or part of the product, you can just present them as a bonus. I'm going to give you the extra thing that cleans the stairs. For example, we worked with someone a while back, was in the bodybuilding space. The book had recipes in it. So we just spun that as a bonus. Instead of making that chapter six of the book, we made the, did the pitch for the core of it, and then we just unbundled the product as it existed and it seemed to help sales. So there's a lot of ways, even if you just, once you think of how to position this, that raise the perceived value, even though the thing you're delivering didn't change. That thing about the recipes is very good because as part of the product, it was kind of an anonymous part. It was something you've talked about, but it wasn't highlighted, making it a bonus, highlighted it, and suddenly it became a very, very valuable piece. So that's, that's judging what you have and figuring out what is the main core of what you offer and then how, how, what is, what are the things that augment that or, or, or compliment it? And then what are the things that are just other things that would make the package better for the prospect? Okay. Just a couple other, I'll put them up as examples that you can comment on of things that we've seen done successfully. What I call the, my word for it is the, the, currently the, the TV infomercial offer. This is, you know, timeless if you watch those late night TV programs, they make the case for the product, whatever the thing happens to be, and they establish a price fairly early on and something you learn about pricing is nobody knows what anything is worth until there's something to compare it to. Right. So whatever the thingy is, the magic cream, the sauce, the box, the, the doodad, it, it, once they, they plant the idea of it's, I'd say, meaning $29, they then, you know, they hit you with two punches. They're, they double the offer, they will now give you twice as much as they established and they cut the price in half or they drop the price. So all along they were planning to sell you this much of this thing for $19, but before they got there, they already planted the seed that I'm, it's half this for twice the price is still a good value. Yeah, that's, that's good salesmanship. That's, that's, that's, as you said, that's establishing the price and then making this a now, now it's not just a lot of, that's a good example of not just a lot of bonuses, you know, you get, we'll send you two of these, we'll send you four of these, but it's also now a bargain. So, and a lot of them also have limited time offers on this, you know, for, if you call now, while this commercial is running, or if you, if you call today, or, you know, when, you know, that comes up, let's talk a little more about that, because people are often concerned, like, what is considered ethical or legal in that space? Like, what if you don't want to update, what if it's not a launch where you're pulling it off the market on Friday and you're like a sell, what if you want to encourage people to act today, but you're not going to update your website tomorrow, you're not going to stop taking phone calls. Where can people be ethically and legally on the right side of that game? A very common blunder that people make is they think that people are operating in a vacuum, that they see your ad on Tuesday and that's all they're going to see and they're not going to see the competition and they're not going to come back on Thursday. So, if you make some kind of case that on Tuesday, this is only for Tuesday and this price is only good for today, Tuesday, January 3rd and then they come back on Thursday and they say you're saying this is only good for Thursday, January 6th, you know, that stuff gets around. So, don't make stupid blunders on this. Don't be so obviously unethical that you get called out for it. There are simple ways to say. If you do this today, you're going to get this particular product. You're not saying it's not going to be available tomorrow, so it may be available tomorrow, but you're making the case. Just do this today. I would like you to do this today. If instead you say do this today or you'll never get another shot at it, that then becomes nonsense if that isn't true. So, don't say stuff that isn't true, but it's perfectly fair to be able to say, I want you to make this deal today. If you do it today, I will give you this, this and this. If you really are going to pull it, and that is one of the beauty of the launch process, which is that you do only have a certain period of time and then it stops. And that's real and they don't extend it. Now, they may do certain windows of extension if there's overwhelming demand or there's some reason to do it, but it's very well explained and it's part of the ongoing process of providing the maximum value you can. But if you say this book is only available this month and then it's not, then make it only available that month. It's okay to reintroduce it later on, but don't be the guy who just makes up stuff because it sounds good. Actually follow through on every promise you make. Yeah, there's, again, because as you've talked about earlier, people that are out to rip people off, they use these techniques. So thus the techniques get tarred or a bad reputation. You don't have to lie if you're in this for the long haul and you have an ethical product. Some people use these strategies and lie. They're, you know, it's the snake oil salesman. They're counting on, they can't be found tomorrow. You don't have to be that person. And if you don't want to be that person, if you are counting on, you want to be found tomorrow, you want to sell more things, you have more products, you just don't need a lie and you're at. You can do all of these things by, the strategies still work by telling the truth. Okay, just one more example, because you talk a little bit about what's called like the fake offer or a false offer, and then we'll wrap this, the Q&A section up. Yeah, sometimes getting the price right in the prospect's head is a matter of just kind of finagling with the various offers. And one of the old school ways of doing this was to have three offers. So you would have like what we call low ball offer, which is if you want to get, if you want to, you know, essentially you say, if you're cheap or if you don't want to spend a lot of money, you can get this kind of basic program for this price. If you want to get the maximum thing, you know, the super-guilded platinum version, then you can get it for this price. Or we have this in between price, which is has a little bit of this and a little bit of that and kind of mixed in. And one of the reasons for that middle price was to position the lower price and position the higher price. And the lower price, the middle price value was almost never a really good deal. It was just a, there was no huge bargain there. There was a huge bargain for the low price deal. It was a great deal if you wanted to just get in on the ground floor. And the maximum deal was a great deal, although it took a lot of money. And then the middle deal was just kind of positioning those. It was just, well, if you don't want to spend a lot, but you don't want to spend the absolute lowest, and you want to get more, you can go to this one. But it was really kind of a positioning price, because most people didn't take the middle one. They would look at it and they would ponder it. But then that helped them say, no, I want to spend a little more and get the really good one. Or, you know, I don't even want to spend that much more and I want to get a small one. And you can make a decision. So the middle one kind of made people fall off the fence one way or the other. Right. Or another way to use that strategy is price the middle one the same as the expensive one. Which thus suddenly makes the expensive one, like, again, it's that comparison, seem like a screaming deal. And generally, when you do good, better, best, there's relatively few better buyers in any market. There's people who want the, get me in the door, basic, people who want the best. So no matter how you spread the pricing, you sell relatively few of the middle. So again, it's the last couple years away, people have leveraged that reality is make what you call the middle one. That's where I got the, I didn't invent the term, but the fake offer or the false offer, where it's a thing they could buy, but no one's going to buy it because for that same amount of money, you get the back daddy with all the bonuses and you know, there's a check in every feature in that call. Okay, I think that takes care of the most common questions, issues people come up with. Again, there's an exercise, John will explain that, and then we'll continue on with the simple writing system. Okay, it's time to do the exercise for this lesson. So basically, what can you say or offer or do that will knock your prospect off the fence appropriate to your market? What kind of bonuses or what kind of actions could you take that will do the job of moving them to the next step, which is getting very, very excited about joining or buying or becoming part of your world?
